coredata.dc:title: Thermo-physical, tribological and machining characteristics of Hastelloy C276 under sustainable cooling/lubrication conditions

coredata.dc:description: Chemical reactivity and poor heat conductivity make nickel (Ni) and its alloys difficult to process. The aerospace industry's expectations for high-quality surfaces are one of the biggest hurdles in machining nickel components. Recent studies have found some flaws in mist lubrication techniques when utilised under high-speed cutting circumstances, thus nanoparticles with higher thermal conductivity than the base fluid is now being used in minimum quantity lubrication (MQL) systems. Nanocarbon dots (CDs) blended with bio lubricant is an effective coolant to enhance the heat transfer capability by decreasing friction. The inclusion of the right nanoparticle ratio to the base liquid is one of the most important challenges that arise when machining. This research focuses on a deeper exploration of Hastelloy C276's tribological behaviour and machinability under various cutting approaches (Dry, MQL Cryo CO2 and N-MQL). Milling trials were carried out with various speed-feed pairings and cutting conditions. Outcomes show that 0.8 wt% CDs with soybean oil reduces the surface roughness by 56–69 %, 15–24 %, and 15–26 % over dry, MQl and cryo CO2 environments. According to elemental study of cutting inserts, adhesion is the primary wear mechanism in all cutting mediums. Finally, the chillness produced by cryo CO2 has a positive effect compared to the N-MQL strategy by producing fine grains.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical reactivity and poor heat conductivity make nickel (Ni) and its alloys difficult to process. The aerospace industry's expectations for high-quality surfaces are one of the biggest hurdles in machining nickel components. Recent studies have found some flaws in mist lubrication techniques when utilised under high-speed cutting circumstances, thus nanoparticles with higher thermal conductivity than the base fluid is now being used in minimum quantity lubrication (MQL) systems. Nanocarbon dots (CDs) blended with bio lubricant is an effective coolant to enhance the heat transfer capability by decreasing friction. The inclusion of the right nanoparticle ratio to the base liquid is one of the most important challenges that arise when machining. This research focuses on a deeper exploration of Hastelloy C276's tribological behaviour and machinability under various cutting approaches (Dry, MQL Cryo CO2 and N-MQL). Milling trials were carried out with various speed-feed pairings and cutting conditions. Outcomes show that 0.8 wt% CDs with soybean oil reduces the surface roughness by 56–69 %, 15–24 %, and 15–26 % over dry, MQl and cryo CO2 environments. According to elemental study of cutting inserts, adhesion is the primary wear mechanism in all cutting mediums. Finally, the chillness produced by cryo CO2 has a positive effect compared to the N-MQL strategy by producing fine grains.

ce:section.ce:para: Nickel-based superalloys (NBS) have been extensively employed in the fabrication of components for high-value industries such as aerospace, nuclear, and marine because of their excellent combination of mechanical qualities at extreme temperatures [1,2]. Unfortunately, these unique attributes reduce their machinability, which has a detrimental effect on the workpiece (w/p) surface as a consequence of the large cutting temperature and the rapid tool wear rate, an outcome of the traditional machining method [3]. The poor machinability deteriorates the mechanical and microstructural properties on the surface, as reported by Liao et al. [4]. Moreover, these characteristic changes might reduce the strength, creep, and fatigue resistance of these superalloys, endangering their trustworthiness in high-stress operations [5].

Introducing cutting fluid (CF) in the cutting region improves the machining ability of difficult-to-machine materials [6–8]. These fluids diminish machining temperature (MT ), friction, and chip removal from the cutting area [9–11]. Thus, it improves the cutting efficiency and component's dimensional accuracy [12–14]. Arunachalam et al. [15] examined the effects of coolant on Inconel machining. Using coolant reduced the built-up edges (BUE), resulting in less compressive or tensile stress. Although the use of CF by the traditional flood cooling approach improved overall global productivity, it had an adverse effect on workers' health and wellbeing [16]. So, from an ecological and economic standpoint, dry cutting, MQL, EMQL and cryogenic (cryo) environments are viable options for reducing this harmful impact on humans and ecosystems [17–19]. The major disadvantage of no coolant condition is the significant amount of heat generated around the cutting area, which reduces cutter life and surface properties of the machined part [19]. Because NBS has poor machinability, MQL's use could be a viable choice for bettering machinability and surface integrity. The MQL process uses less CF, typically 10–500 ml/h. The CF is combined with compressed air and allowed to deliver in the cutting area in the form of microdroplets [20]. This procedure considerably decreases the environmental effect by vaporising the microdroplets using latent heat extracted from the cutting zone [21]. Additionally, utilising this technique results in lower costs of production, lowered energy consumption, and more operator safety [22]. Ghatge et al. [23] examined the influence of mineral and vegetable oil (VO) on MQL machining to improve the machinability. Compared to mineral oil, VO reduced MT, flank wear, and surface topography. Khaliq et al. [24] demonstrated that MQL fashioned better surface trait and longer tool life than dry cutting while micro-milling of titanium alloy. While using various MQL conditions, optimising the process parameters is required to make the best use of the resources. In MQL aided hard turning, Szczotkarz et al. [25] analysed the effect of cooling conditions on tool wear while machining SS316 steel under MQL conditions.. MQL improved the machinability by successfully postponing chips adhering to the cutting tool's edge [26,27]. While MQL has several advantages, such as superior thermal stability and lubricating qualities than other cutting environments, it has a significant disadvantage in terms of cooling capacity [28]. As a consequence of rising cutting temperature at high velocities, the majority of the oil microparticles evaporate before reaching the cutting zone, resulting in poor cooling and lubrication. Electrostatic Minimum Quantity Lubrication (EMQL) technology is developed to circumvent these constraints [29]. By merging two technologies, “Electrostatic Spraying” and MQL, mist particles are electrically charged before reaching the cutting area in this procedure. EMQL's finer and more regulated oil particles improve machinability [30]. Based on tribological performance, the optimum voltage for EMQL within the range of 0–25 kV has been determined. The EMQL at optimized electrostatic voltage resulted in 38 % decreased tool wear as compared to conventional MQL for 2400 mm cutting length [31]. Lv et al. [32] investigated the cutting capability of EMQL using vegetable oil as the cutting fluid. They discovered that EMQL may reduce PM10 and PM2.5 concentrations to 2.4 mg/m3 and 1.0 mg/m3, respectively. However, the concentrations of PM10 and PM2.5 in the oil-based cutting fluids utilised in the EMQL system were challenging to fulfil the NOISH concentration criteria.

Cryo cooling has been used to improve the surface behaviour by lowering the working area heat while cutting NBS. The larger part of cryo cooling research has concentrated on the turning process, which uses various workpiece materials such as Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718, and Nimonic. However, research on cryogenic cooling in NBS milling is minimal. Liquid nitrogen (LN2) and liquid carbon dioxide (CO2 ) are notable cryogens [33]. Varghese et al. [34] compared the benefits of LN2 environment to disparate cooling methods engaging carbide inserts. LN2's chilling action lowers adhesive wear as much as achievable, saving power and making the operation more cost-effective. When milling Udimet alloy, Cryo LN2 significantly outperforms oil-based coolant by raising the speed from 10 m/min to 120 m/min [35]. Ross and Manimaran [17] studied the results of cutting Nimonic-80A with coated cutter under various L/C techniques. Roughness was reduced by 42–47 % under dry condition, 24–27 % under flood condition, and 16–21 % under MQL condition in relation to CO2 cooling. The accomplishment of the CO2 cooling approach under a 45° nozzle angle was examined. CO2 as a coolant extends tool life to the greatest degree possible concerning different cooling settings, and the 45o nozzle angle allows the cryogen to reach efficiently to the contact region (K. Nimel Sworna Ross [36]). Even though cryogenic environments have been investigated for greater performance, inconsistent results have also been reported as a reason for poor lubrication.

Yildirim et al. [37] made a claim, stating that MQL fell short under severe machining conditions in terms of cooling. To solve the issue in MQL, researchers turned to nanofluids (NFs) as their primary focus to increase the cooling capacity. NFs are made by mixing solid nanoparticles (NPs) with CF to improve the cutting oil's characteristics. The heat transfer and tribological characteristics of the CFs to which NPs are introduced are improved by the addition of NPs. CFs with a higher thermal conductivity are more efficient at removing heat from the cutting area. Furthermore, as tribological qualities increase, a more effective oil layer forms at the work-cutter junction [38]. The MT is restricted from distributing to the tool and w/p in this manner, and significant gain in tool life can be made [39]. The main subgroups of nanosized particles are inorganic or organic composition. Due to a huge quantity of investigations covering solid distribution in the pure state or multicomponent molecular structure, inorganic NPs appear to be dominating the scenario at the moment [40]. The comprehensive review work by Kulkarni et al. [41] demonstrates that organic NPs, namely carbon nanostructures with different lattice arrangements like graphene, carbon nanotubes, graphite, nano diamonds and carbon dots [42] have diverse functions depending on their dimensionality, but majority of them exhibit a significant increase in thermal conductivity. Depending on the properties of the nano-material, nanoparticles can make the base liquid much stronger in terms of thermal conductivity. NFs can also benefit from the addition of surfactants. Gong et al. [43] assessed the tool wear and roughness in Inconel 718 cutting by employing dry, pure MQL, and graphene-enhanced N-MQL. When compared to previous techniques, they discovered that N-MQL enhanced the processing performance. Yi et al. [44], under flood and graphene oxide-enhanced N-MQL, Ti–6Al–4 V was drilled. The researchers stated that adding graphene oxide to the N-MQL system increased machining effectiveness. Using a multilayer (TiCN/Al2O3/TiN) coated tool, the investigation examined the surface trait facets of white layer formation, microhardness and residual stress in finish hard turning of AISI D3 steel under nano MQL (graphene NPs with enriched radiator oil) environment. With NF machining, the L/C was shown to be successful, safe and environmentally friendly, as well as assisting in the improvement of sustainability [45]. Kıvak et al. [46] turned hard Ni material with dry, MQL, graphene + MQL and MWCNT + MQL cutting strategies, researchers looked at surface roughness, MT, and tool life. They proclaimed that graphene + MQL cutting offered better outcomes in regards to surface waviness and MT. Sarıkaya et al. [47] investigated the effects of distinct nano MQL cutting environments in the turning of Co-based Haynes 25. According to their experimental results, hBN-N-MQL was successful in restricting notch wear.

More research is needed to study the impact of coolants on NBS machinability to increase their functional performance, as discussed in the literature above. Furthermore, the impact of CF on tool wear and surface morphological behaviour has yet to be fully explored and there is no clue on cutting Hastelloy C276 using carbon dots (CDs) mixed with soybean oil. Hence, in this work, to improve the machinability of Hastelloy C276, distinct coolants (dry, MQL, CO2 and N-MQL) are employed and their overall performance is analysed in terms cutting temperature, surface behaviour, tool wear, morphology of chips, microstructure and microhardness. In addition, electron dispersion spectroscopy (EDS) was performed to confirm the sticking of elements on the tool insert.

NFs were prepared by using the two-step method because of its advantage. Initially, the green synthesis of CDs was carried out from Hibiscus sabdariffa by hydrothermal technique [48]. The specific quantity of green leaves, washed, ground, and extracted with 150 ml of water. The resulting extract was autoclaved at 160 °C for 8 h. After the liquid had cooled, it was spun in a centrifuge to get rid of larger particles. The liquid was then freeze-dried to make CDs. Soybean oil (SO) is a promising environmentally friendly, sustainable, and biodegradable alternative to mineral oil–based emulsion flood cooling strategies. [49]. SO is mainly composed of fatty acid and triglyceride, -COOH in the fatty acid molecules and -COOR in triglyceride both belong to polar groups, which gives them excellent lubrication property [50]. The NF (CDs + SO) was prepared in different wt. percentages ranging from 0.1 to 1.0 % with a 0.1 % increment of CDs. From the previous investigations, the wt. percentage was varied.

Chemicals (surfactants) may be added to the CDs + SO combination during the preparation process to generate a homogenous mixture and reduce surface tension. The use of surfactant results in a homogenous mixture by maintaining the NPs floating and prevents agglomeration, resulting in mixes that are stable for a long period. Cetrimonium bromide is used as a surfactant in this investigation. The surfactant rate of 0.02 wt% was used in this research work in relation with previous investigations. To prepare CDs + SO, the green synthesized CDs were mixed with SO and sonicated (Qsonica Sonicator) for about 30 mins. To ensure complete homogenization the mixture was further stirred with a magnetic stirrer (Relitech) for about 1 h. Schematic representation for preparation of CDs + SO is given in Fig. 1 . The Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs and their particle size is presented in Fig. 2 .

The dispersion of CDs with SO was investigated using a UV–visible spectrophotometric analysis. The wavelength of maximum absorption (λmax) is determined using a dilute solution of CDs and SO (1:5). In addition, the concentration dependence of absorbance on λmax was investigated in order to study the dispersion stability. The thermal conductivity of oil determines its ability to transfer heat. The thermal conductivity of CDs with SO was determined with a help of thermal property analyser (TEMPOS). The viscosity of CDs blended with SO was tested using a Rheometer (NETZSCH-make and Kinexus-DSR- model), it is a significant factor in determining its lubricating characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cutting conditions, material and machine tool details
ce:section.ce:para:
The trials were experimented by employing Hastelloy C276 with the dimension of 100 × 60 × 10 mm3 by variety of coolants (Dry, MQL, CO2 and N-MQL) at varied speed-feed combo to study the surface behaviour. Tables 1 and 2
show the Hastelloy C276's constituent composition as well as its physical, mechanical and thermal properties. The milling experiments were carried out on a HAAS-VF2 milling machine with a spindle power of 22.4 kW and a top speed of 8100 rpm. When milling, TaeguTec's PVD-TiAlN coated carbide inserts with the YG-1 APMT1135 specification were employed. These inserts were mounted to a TaeguTec tool holder with the model number BAP 300R C12-12-130-1 T. The 30 mm nozzle distance and 45o nozzle angle were sustained based on existing literatures for all cutting environments. Fig. 3 depicts the experimental setup for the research. As per the previous studies and manufacturer's specification, machining parameters were chosen and shown in Table 3 .

Dry, MQL, CO2, and N-MQL are the four strategies examined in this article. In the absence of a coolant (dry environment), no CF was utilised to cool the cutter-work material contact. A nozzle, mixing chamber, fluid intake, compressor, and fluid sump seem to be the most essential components of MQL functioning. The droplets were shot to fall on the rake side of the cutter at a 45° angle with a 0.5–5 μm droplet size. In a cryo tank, CO2 was compressed at a tremendous pressure (57 bar) and a temperature of 20 °C. Because of the Joule–Thomson effect, liquid CO2 becomes 40 % snow and 60 % gas as it exits the pipe. Without any external energy, the cryogen is blasted aggressively at the cutting region (rake face) due to the pressure inside the cryo tank. The same system utilised for MQL was employed for the NF application.

Other phenomena, in addition to adequate viscosity and specific heat capacity, may improve the effectiveness of NFs. The difficulties and limits involved with characterisation in testing play a part in identifying the mechanisms involved in the increased thermal capabilities of NFs. The mechanism is explained in Fig. 4 .
(i)
Rolling mechanism

The NF's well-distributed spherical NPs can shift from sliding to rolling friction, which affects the friction mode. Also, the load-bearing ability has been increased. According to the rolling mechanism concept, two aspects pay attention to NF's superior lubricating effect. One is that NPs are normally spherical and can operate as a “micro-bearing” during friction; the other is that at high temperatures and loads, NPs amid two rubbing surfaces turn flat and create a “sliding arrangement” that drops wear and friction.
(ii)
Tribo-film mechanism

NPs that are widely dispersed in NFs are likely to form a thin film layer on the metal surface, separating the rubbing surface and lowering friction. In the process of friction, the NPs in the NF tend to migrate to the rubbing surface due to distinct factors. Tribo-film action is the most widely accepted hypothesis for NFs' anti-wear and friction reduction.
(iii)
Self-repairing mechanism

NPs in NFs incline to patch microcracks and heal micro damages through deposition on the rubbing surface, ultimately assisting in the formation of more perfectly smooth surfaces. It is important to note that the “self-repairing” mechanism is more than just a build-up of NPs on the rubbing surface. The melting points of NPs decrease significantly as their size decreases. At high rubbing surface temperatures, these NPs are easily sintered in the contact area, sequential filler is generated and tightly bound to the rubbing surface.
(iv)
Polishing effect

The mechanical polishing of rubbing surfaces by friction is facilitated by the hard NPs in NFs, which operate as a great polishing tool. The smoother surface polished with hard NPs increases the true contacting area of tribo-pairs, increasing load-carrying capacity and decreasing friction coefficient. They are capable of repairing the surface on an atomic scale.

The amount of heat generated when cutting was recorded with an infrared instrument (HTC brand) over a temperature range of −50 to 1650oC (1 %). To produce a somewhat close result, the emissivity of the IR thermometer is tuned to 0.95–0.97. The mean roughness value is used in this study since it is widely accepted (>50 %) in industrial areas. The SE-3500 roughness tester was utilised to measure machined surface roughness for distinct cutting conditions. Before every trial, the instrument was calibrated using slip gauges to assure accuracy. The insert was detached after every test to examine flank side wear. A Video measurement device was employed to assess the cutter wear (VMD-2010F). The flaws in the machined region, wear mechanism, and chip morphology were investigated using an EVO 18 scanning electron microscope (SEM). An optical microscope with a resolution of 1000× was used to examine the microstructure of the sliced surfaces. The average grain size was determined using the ASTM E112-13 standard. Tests of microhardness were performed on a Mitutoyo (940-130) microhardness measuring equipment with a load of 150 g and a dwell time of 10 s. The results were recorded at 3 different places in accordance with ASTM E 384-17 standards.

The effective dispersion of CDs onto the SO was investigated using a UV–Visible spectral technique. Surfactants were used to prevent agglomeration. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the maximum wavelength of absorption for CDs-SO was found to be 450 nm. In addition, absorbance was recorded for varying concentration (0.1 to 1.0 %) of CDs + SO. Absorbance originally increased with an increase in the concentration of carbon dots up to 0.8 % (Fig. 5b). Whereas absorbance decreased notably beyond 0.8 %, this may be attributed to sedimentation of CDs with increased concentration. Henceforth optimum weight % of CDs were fixed to 0.8 % for SO.

Thermal conductivity of SO and CDs blended SO is investigated to evaluate the heat transferability. CDs + SO possessed high thermal conductivity in comparison with base oil. Thermal conductivity was initiate to rise up to 0.8 % of CDs concentration, further increase in the concentration of CDs showed decreased conductivity, which is ascribed to agglomeration of CDs at higher concentrations. Fig. 6a illustrates the variation of conductivity concerning wt% of CDs blended SO.

The viscosity of oil plays a major role in reducing the internal friction force. Formerly, moderate viscosity is expected for better lubrication purposes. In view of this, the viscosity of varying wt. percentage of CDs + SO is determined and the results are depicted in Fig. 6b. As the concentration increases viscosity increases to 0.8 % of CDs. When the concentration is increased beyond this, the viscosity starts decreasing. Owing to the fact that sedimentation of particles leads to ineffective dispersion results at higher concentrations. As likely viscosity of SO was also found to decrease with temperature rise. This is because at a higher temperature the intermolecular forces are weakened due to high thermal energy.

The L/C technique is essential to provide an effective machining operation [45]. The convective heat transfer that occurs throughout the cutting process aids in the evacuation of heat from the main cutting region [51,52]. Fig. 7 illustrates the MT formed at cutter-work material junction under disparate cutting strategies. With a N-MQL cooling environment, a minimum cutting temperature was obtained at all values of Vc. Under CDs assisted MQL machining condition, cutting temperature was substantially reduced.

The heat in the cutting area is a key property that has a real effect on w/p dimensional accuracy and cutter wear mechanics [53]. MT is especially significant in complex procedures performed in a semi-closed environment, such as milling. The produced heat is highly dependent on machining circumstances and material characteristics. It is thought to fluctuate correspondingly with Eq. (1).
(1)
T = u
Vc × f
k × ρ × c

T is the formed heat when cutting, whereas, u, ρ, k, and c specify the cutting energy, density, specific heat and thermal conductivity of the w/p. Described material features, specifically thermal conductivity, which contributes to temperature rise. With good thermal conductivity, milling of ferrous material shows considerably low temperature both under with and without coolant [54]; Shearing heat dissipates quickly in the environment. The higher the thermal conductivity of a material, the more heat it can move through it [46]. Less heat was created at the cutter-work material junction because the lubricant lowered the frictional heating factors. Nonetheless, due to Hastelloy's weak thermal conductivity, heat does not drain effectively into the w/p while cutting [55]. Particularly, low thermal conductivity is largely to blame for the elevated MT that is generated during the machining processes. The cutting tool's lifespan is shortened as a result of increased temperature. It is necessary to keep the MT under control during the milling operation. Fig. 7 shows the MT of Hastelloy C276 at a distinct feed-speed combo under varied cutting environments. The low temperature (61 °C) was gotten with CDs with MQL at a Vc of 60 m/min and a f of 0.08 mm/rev, and as expected, the highest (291 °C) was obtained with dry cutting at a f of 0.12 mm/rev and a Vc of 90 m/min. At a Vc of 90 m/min and a f of 0.12 mm/rev, the MT produced are 291 °C, 146 °C, 119 °C and 97 °C for dry, MQL, CO2 and N-MQL, respectively. The MT showed a 67 %, 34 % and 18 % decrease with N-MQL cutting concerning dry, MQL and CO2 strategies. The overall percentage reduction was found to be 67–76 % for dry cutting, 33–42 % for MQL and 16–28 % for CO2 over N-MQL environment. The mechanisms discussed in Section 2.3 are most likely to be responsible for the temperature drop when cutting with CDs + MQL. The evaluation of NFs shows better heat transfer in relation to other cutting environments. Surfactants added to NFs are to enable the homogeneous suspension of NPs. Any additive added to the NF has an effect on the tribofilm layer created in the cutting region. According to prior research, viscosity and wettability play a multi-function in declining the friction at the cutting region. The matching thing was explained by Gunan et al. [56].

The unevenness of the machined face has an impact on working performance, cutter-work material interaction, and the life of the machined product [57]. As a result, it is one of the most important criteria in the machining industry [58,59]. Ra values are influenced by several elements such as cutting tool design, number of coating layers, and lubrication media [60]. However, in the current study, the effects of varied environments on Ra were investigated with a disparate speed-feed combo. Vc and Ra, as shown in Fig. 8 , are inversely proportional; that is, as Vc increases, roughness decreases. The f is a function of Ra, changes in the f value have a major impact on Ra. As the temperature is raised during milling, the work material softens and the cutting force and vibration are reduced.

The Ra values achieved for dry, MQL, CO2, and N-MQL conditions were 1.63 μm, 0.73 μm, 0.68 μm, and 0.58 μm, respectively, at a Vc of 90 m/min and a f of 0.12 mm/rev. In regard to dry, MQL, and cryogenic conditions, N-MQL reduced roughness by 64 %, 21 %, and 15 %, respectively. In this case, it is clear that N-MQL entered successfully through the tool-work material border, thereby reducing unevenness. Previous research has demonstrated that NFs have an outstanding capacity to lessen the imperfection of the machined area [61]. Additionally, the researchers demonstrated that NPs infused VO medium produce superior surface morphology related to alternative lubricating media. The maximum Ra was found here by machining in a dry environment. In terms of dry, MQL, and cryogenic strategies, the Ra values tested under 0.8 wt% CDs + SO showed a 56–69 %, 15–24 %, and 15–26 % decline, respectively.

Fig. 9 displays an SEM examination and 3D topography of the milled surfaces at a Vc of 90 m/min and a f of 0.1 mm/rev. Rough marks on the machined side, material deposition, and smearing were seen on the surface of the milled face under a dry medium. The lowness and peaks in the surface topography decrease the surface quality by generating protrusions and indentations. Although MQL provides a superior surface trait than dry cutting, it still falls short concerning cooling performance. It is important to note that in the non-appearance of any oil, CO2, which is dry, clean and inert, shows obvious feed marks on the milled face. However, it generates a decent surface when compared to MQL and dry conditions. Smooth surfaces were obtained when CDs were paired with MQL. Furthermore, as shown by the 3D topographies of the surfaces, there is a clear reduction in the lowness and peaks when using CO2 and N-MQL strategies. CDs enhanced SO has a higher viscosity than conventional SO in MQL, allowing for the long-term persistence of a nano-green combination in the machining region, as previously mentioned (Section 3.2). NPs filling microchannels on the machined surface are another possible justification for better surface quality.

Material damage in the area of contact amid cutter-work material is referred to as cutting tool wear, and it is the most fundamental indicator of a tool's lifespan [62,63]. When Vc rises, the tool life diminishes, which leads to greater insert wear [64]. The flank wear criteria of the tool wear was based on 0.3 mm as in the literature studies. Many indices of a machining process are affected by tool wear, including w/p surface integrity, cutting force, energy usage, and so on. The variation in roughness is presented in Fig. 10 . At a Vc of 90 m/min and a f of 0.12 mm/rev, the Vb values for dry, MQL, CO2, and N-MQL conditions were 0.148 mm, 0.103 mm, 0.088 mm, and 0.073 mm, respectively. In regard to dry, MQL, and cryogenic conditions, N-MQL reduced roughness by 51 %, 29 %, and 17 %, respectively. The most important decision of this study is that a small amount of VO is more effective than cryogens in minimising tool wear at lower Vc. However, as Vc is raised, MQL loses its lubricating property due to rising heat. Before it reaches the work-cutter junction, the oil droplets evaporate. By lowering the junction temperature, the cooling impact of CO2 reduces Vb to its maximum extent. Cryogen used forced convection to evacuate the cutting temperature. MQL has two significant qualities. 1. frictional impact is reduced. 2. lowers the temperature (at low speed). CDs were introduced with MQL to improve effective heat transfer. The success of this N-MQL method is due to the fact that the NPs lessen the temperature of the MQL fluid. As a result, the droplets do not vaporize despite of high heat in the cutting area [65].

The cutter was damaged by means of tiny chips, which were ultimately responsible for the inserts being rendered unusable. It is evident that abrasion and adhesion are the primary mechanisms of wear [66] under all lubrication circumstances. After a certain point, MT continues to grow, smaller chips become increasingly glued to the tool. As a result, BUE is generated on the rake face. Abrasive wear is just another category of wear that happens when cutting NBS. Ploughing was seen parallel to chip flow direction. Hard abrasive particles present in NBS enters the cutter-work material junction and produce a ploughing effect. Excessive cutting speed exacerbates the problem.

Fig. 11 displays the SEM micrographs of the rake (left) and flank (right) sides. BUE provides justification for adhesive wear. As can be seen, Ni is most adherent to the tool surface during dry cutting, followed by abrasion, ploughing, and coating delamination [3]. The mass of Ni adhered to the coating increases the tangential shear stress that causes abrasion and delamination. Effectively, deposits of w/p material on the rake side close to the cutting tip reveal chip rubbing. The lubricating effect in the cutting area minimized tool wear in the MQL environment. This could be related to a reduction in MT, which lowered BUE formation to a certain extent. Ploughing along the cutting edge implies that the load is sometimes greater than the w/p strength. Furthermore, tungsten carbide was exposed in a few places, and a minor amount of BUE was discovered at the cutting tip. In the instance of cryo cutting, the phenomenon of abrasion is higher as demonstrated in Fig. 11. As a result, when cutting with CO2, adhesive wear remains in the tool.

Undeniably, even if cryo aid can greatly lower the cutting zone's heat, its poor lube property affects the coating and burns on the rake surface were seen. Temperature levels at the tooltip are still vital and wear remain to involve even if it has been reduced down by the action of the cryo CO2. Fig. 12 depicts the EDX of the milling cutter after machining the w/p under N-MQL. Adhesion is seen on the rake side of the insert. Though, similar to the technique used in the MQL strategy, N-MQL has greatly reduced the creation of BUEs. Furthermore, when milling Hastelloy under N-MQL, a less defected cutting tip was attained in relation to dry cutting, MQL, and CO2 conditions. The NPs on hold in the base fluid improved the L/C properties while cutting. Nano-sized particles enhanced the fluid's tribological assets by exhibiting a ball effect at the work-cutter junction. Because of their varied molecular structures, NPs improve the lubricating performance. NPs have varying chemical activity, they produce a variety of film formation processes and antifriction qualities in the cutting region. In combination to the tribological qualities achieved with NFs, the compressed air used in the MQL and N-MQL systems facilitated the rapid discharge of chips from the cutting region, which contributed to adhesion reduction. In a nutshell, alternative L/C medium can lessen the tool wear but not modify the type of wear. Concerning all the cutting strategies employed in this investigation, the tool wear mechanisms have been discussed with the help of SEM.

The EDS analysis obtained during all the cutting strategies is displayed in Fig. 12. EDS analysis represents the adhered material consisted of Ni and other few elements of Hastelloy under no coolant condition. The adhesion mechanism establishes BUE formation that protects the rake face. However, when achieving a stagnation state, the BUE induces the peeling of tool coating material [64]. The EDS analysis likewise reveals the presence of w/p particles in the tool material at a lower percentage than in dry cutting when machining with MQL. Cryo media have been demonstrated in previous research to be unsuccessful at dipping the abrasive wear due to their low lubricating capabilities. EDS analysis also shows adhesion phenomena under CO2 cutting. The degradation of the tool was primarily due to adhesion under all the cutting environments as a reason for poor thermal conductivity. The same trend was observed by Jamil et al. [25] when machining titanium alloys under distinct L/C conditions. N-MQL shows a very less quantity of w/p material on the insert concerning other cutting strategies used in this study.

Following the milling process, the gathered chips were examined under a SEM to explore the deformation behaviour. The influence of coolant methods was explored by evaluating the shape of chips. Fig. 13 depicts the chip shape produced at a f of 0.12 mm/rev across varied cooling environments. Explaining the chip structure and morphology is essential for assessing and improving a material's workability and surface trait [67]. In general, two hypotheses exist to explain the production of jagged chips: the adiabatic shear theory and the periodic crack theory. The first is generated by periodic thermoplastic shear instability in the chip forming zone. In all coolant environments, the machining of Hastelloy yielded jagged chips. Similar chips were also found in difficult-to-machine materials [68]. It is due to thermally induced deformation in the primary cutting area, that causes shear volatility and shear localization. The jagged chip generation also results in higher power consumption and lower surface quality. Large jagged chips were observed in the dry and MQL modes; moderate was observed in the CO2 environment, while it was smaller in the N-MQL mode. Examining the SEM pictures, it is evident that the teeth are significantly sharper in no coolant condition than in the other cutting medium. Even though superior lubrication with MQL and a cooling impact from a cryogenic strategy have not been effective in limiting friction. The introduction of CDs with MQL reduced the friction as a reason of proper L/C. So, there might be a link amid tooth formation and surface superiority.

NBS can keep their mechanical qualities even at high temperatures. The machined face's microstructure with distinct cooling processes is depicted in Fig. 14 . The adding of hardness to the machined zone's sub-surface was done for a diverse of reasons, lowering of thermal softening, refined grains, and strain hardening [43]. The grains on the machined face cooled quickly due to convective heat transfer either by natural convection or forced convection. The quick heating-cooling of the CO2 strategy leads to a finer grain layer. Hardness is usually affected by grain refining. Although N-MQL harvests superior surface quality, it has fewer compact grains when compared to CO2. The diameter of the grains has a massive effect on their ductility and fatigue life. Grain boundary sliding can result from high temperatures, leading to the creation of voids along the boundaries [69]. The force of the tool is applied to the material's surface, producing grain fragmentation [70]. The formed grain structure developed in the Cryo setting is finer than that developed in dry, MQL, and N-MQL milling environments. The negative temperature coolant lowers the temperature in the cutting area, giving rise to fine grain size. The area of refined grain layer created after machining with Dry, MQL, CO2, and N-MQL cutting regimes are presented in Fig. 15 . In comparison to other cutting environments, the grain size obtained with CO2 was relatively low at a Vc of 90 m/min. A noteworthy alteration has been detected with Dry (150–265 μm2), MQL (85–225 μm2), CO2 (65–205 μm2) and N-MQL (75–220 μm2) environments. A portion of the heat generated during milling is transferred to the milled surface. The speedy heating-cooling cycle with cryo lessens the size of the grain area.

The majority of cutting alters the working materials sub-surface [20]. A multitude of parameters, including the machining environment, tool diameter, and machining inputs, influence the hardness of the sub-surface [71]. In the perspective of bulk hardness, the machined side displays an increase in hardness as a consequence of rapid heating-cooling. The microhardness profiles for milling Hastelloy C276 with different L/C environments are plotted in Fig. 16 . The machined face has a greater degree of microhardness. The hardness formed at the machined surface was 271 Hv, 281 Hv, 299 Hv, 285 Hv for dry, MQL, CO2 and N-MQL, respectively. Due to cryo CO2's quick heating-cooling impact, it results in an increase in micro-hardness when compared to other cutting techniques. Micro-hardness measures appear to be more responsive to grain size [72]. The microhardness values declined with distance from the machined face in all of the experiments. Microhardness is found to be higher in compacted grains with small sizes, which is consistent with Srinivasan et al. [73] findings.

A CDs-NF was created and characterised with variable concentration. The concentrations of the generated nano oils were optimized using their absorbance, thermal conductivity, and viscosity. Experiments on milling were carried out in a dry, MQL cryogenic, and 0.8 % CDs fortified oil media. The following are the results:
•
Pure SO has an absorbance of 0.54, according to spectrophotometer analysis, and the absorbance potential steadily increases as CDs are deposited. At 0.8 wt% CDs concentration, the reinforcement had reached its saturation stage. The addition of NPs greatly improves the thermal conductivity and dynamic viscosity of SO.
•
At all degrees of cutting speed, a minimum cutting temperature was obtained with N-MQL environment. Under CDs with oil condition, the reduction was found to be 67–76 % against dry cutting, 33–42 % against MQL and 16–28 % over CO2 environment. Based on these outcomes, it is strong that NPs reduce the friction at the cutting area effectively.
•
The roughness was least under N-MQL at all stages of the speed-feed combo. Under the N-MQL cutting method, the SEM and 3D surface topography analyses revealed a flat surface with fewer lowness and peaks. It is due to the fact that N-MQL provides both L/C actions at the cutter-work material junction.
•
The Vb decreases when the N-MQL cooling system is used in the cutting process. The considerable reduction is due to the cutting region's superior heat dissipation, which significantly reduced the heat and friction at the cutter-work material junction
•
The BUE formations are visible in all cutting environments, according to the detailed investigation of damaged tools employing SEM and EDS. BUE generation is much reduced as compared to dry cutting, however partial adhesions were still observable when machined in the N-MQL environment.
•
In general, jagged chips were formed when machining NBS, especially in dry cutting conditions; however, the chips generated by N-MQL are smooth, therefore jagged teeth are not evident. This is due to the N-MQL's greater L/C mechanism at elevated temperatures.
•
The fast heating and cooling of the Cryo technique reduces the size of grains. Finally, under the Cryo environment, a substantial increase in micro-hardness was observed.
